context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.
exercise:
Course context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.

Exercise:
When is it morally okay to kill one person so as to prevent five people from being killed? How would an act consequentialist think about this question? What would an act consequentialist say about the cases we considered in Lecture 13 (e.g. Fuel, Blood and the different trolley cases)? Does the act consequentialist get these cases right? If not, what is the right general account of when it is morally okay to kill one person so as to save five?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m on this MIT OCW philosophy prompt about when it’s morally okay to kill 1 to prevent 5 deaths. Can you just write me a submission-ready answer that explains the act consequentialist view and applies it to Fuel, Blood, and the trolley cases, and then says whether that view gets them right?
exchanges.tutor:
I can’t write a submission-ready answer for you, but I can help you build your own. Let’s start by pinning down the core principle.

1) In one sentence, what is the act consequentialist test for whether an action is morally right?

2) For each case (Fuel, Blood, and ONE trolley variant to start), list: (a) the options the agent has, (b) the consequences of each option (who lives/dies), and (c) which option act consequentialism would pick. What do you get for the first case you choose?

3) Then: do you personally think that verdict is correct? If not, what feature seems morally relevant (e.g., doing vs allowing, intending vs foreseeing, rights, consent, fairness)? Pick ONE feature you think matters most and say why.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will refuse to write a submission-ready response (the student must do their own writing). To support learning and comply with the rules, I’ll use Socratic, guided-discovery questions that lead the student to: (1) state the act consequentialist decision rule, (2) apply it case-by-case (Fuel, Blood, trolley variants) by identifying options and outcomes, and (3) evaluate whether the theory’s verdicts match the student’s considered judgments and motivate a possible “right general account.” I’ll keep it bite-sized and focused, asking for the student’s own drafts/claims to respond to rather than providing a full solution.

exchanges.student: I get the basic thing is “pick the act with the best overall consequences,” but I really need the exact one-sentence principle you want me to use, plus the verdicts for Fuel, Blood, and the main trolley variants (switch, push/fat man, etc.) spelled out clearly so I can paste them in. Also can you tell me what the “best” objection is (doing vs allowing / intent vs foresight / rights) and the clean alternative account I should conclude with?
exchanges.tutor:
I can’t give you paste-ready sentences, verdicts, or a “best objection/alternative account.” That would be me doing the assignment. But you’re close—let’s make you generate them quickly.

A) Write YOUR one-sentence act consequentialist principle (no jargon needed). Aim for: “An act is right iff …” What’s your draft?

B) For each scenario, fill this 3-line template (you can do it in bullets):
1) Options: (what can the agent do?)
2) Outcomes: (how many die/live under each option?)
3) Act-consequentialist choice: (which option has the best outcome?)

Start with ONE trolley variant (Switch). What do you get for options/outcomes/choice?

C) Now the evaluation move: which pair of cases *seems* to produce the most uncomfortable conflict for act consequentialism in your own judgment—(Switch vs Push) or (Trolley vs Blood) or (Fuel vs Blood)? Pick one pair and tell me (in a sentence) what feels different morally.

Once you answer A–C, I’ll help you sharpen (i) the exact principle wording you’re using, (ii) the case verdicts you’ve derived, and (iii) which objection your own judgments actually support—without me handing you a prefab conclusion.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will not provide paste-ready principles, verdict lists, “best objection,” or a clean alternative account, because that would amount to supplying the student’s solution. Instead I’ll keep the student doing the work by (1) having them propose their own one-sentence principle in their own words, (2) guiding them to derive verdicts by filling a simple options→outcomes table for each case and variant, and (3) prompting them to compare those verdicts to their considered judgments to generate an objection and a revised account. I’ll constrain the task to bite-sized steps and ask for their first attempt so I can critique and steer without giving answers directly.

exchanges.student:
Okay, my draft principle: “An act is morally right iff it produces the best overall outcome (maximizes total well-being / minimizes total harm) compared to the alternatives.”

For Switch trolley: options are (1) do nothing → 5 die, (2) pull the switch → 1 dies; so an act consequentialist picks pulling the switch.

The conflict that bugs me most is Switch vs Push: in Switch it feels okay to redirect to kill 1, but in Push it feels wrong to shove someone to stop the trolley even though the numbers are the same—can you help me articulate what morally relevant difference that is supposed to be?
exchanges.tutor:
Your principle is in good shape. Two quick tightening questions before we move on:

1) When you say “best overall outcome,” are you treating it as *just* body-count/well-being totals, or do you think things like rights/intent count in the “outcome” too? Pick one.

On Switch vs Push, here are the standard candidate differences—choose ONE you think is doing the real work, then defend it:

A) Means vs side-effect: in Push the person’s death is used as a tool; in Switch it’s a foreseen side-effect.
B) Intention: in Push you intend the person’s death; in Switch you intend to save five and merely foresee one death.
C) Doing vs allowing / directness: Push is a more direct killing; Switch is redirecting an existing threat.
D) Rights/agency: Push treats the person as a mere means/violates a stringent right not to be killed.

Which letter best matches your own reaction, and why (one or two sentences)?

Then we’ll pressure-test it: if your chosen difference is the right one, what would it predict about this variant—“Loop” trolley (where turning the trolley leads it onto a loop and it stops because it hits the one)? Would that feel more like Switch or more like Push to you, and why?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a usable principle and one case derivation. I’ll validate the structure but push precision (what counts as “overall outcome,” tie-breaking, agent-relative concerns). Then I’ll use Socratic prompts to get them to articulate candidate morally relevant differences between Switch and Push (means vs side-effect, intention, personal force, rights), and to test each candidate for consistency by applying it to nearby variants. I will not supply the “correct” distinction; I’ll offer a short menu of common candidates and ask the student to choose and defend one with a simple consistency check.

exchanges.student:
I think “best overall outcome” for act consequentialism is basically just totals (deaths/well-being), not rights/intent built in.

For Switch vs Push, I’m going with A/B (means vs side-effect / intention): in Push the person’s death is the mechanism that saves the five, while in Switch the one dies as a foreseen byproduct of rerouting the trolley.

For Loop, I guess it starts feeling more like Push because the one’s death is what stops the trolley, so it’s not just a side-effect anymore—can you help me phrase that cleanly and then tell me how an act consequentialist would respond without sounding totally insane?
exchanges.tutor:
You’ve got the idea; you just need to state it as a claim + reason.

Try drafting TWO sentences using this structure (in your own words):
1) “Loop is morally closer to ___ than to ___ because …”
2) “The key difference is that in Loop the death of the one is ___ to saving the five, whereas in Switch it is only ___.”

Now, for the act consequentialist response, don’t make them “sound sane” by adding rights/intent—they won’t. Instead, make them consistent. Two guiding questions:

- If act consequentialism only cares about totals, what should it say about Switch vs Push vs Loop when the outcomes are 5 saved/1 killed in each?
- If you want a *more sophisticated* consequentialist reply, what kinds of longer-run consequences might differ between permitting Push and permitting Switch (think: fear, trust, predictability, incentives, risk of abuse, errors)?

Write your two-sentence Loop distinction draft + a 1–2 sentence consequentialist reply draft, and I’ll help you tighten the logic (without giving you wording to paste).
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: Student is asking for phrasing and for a consequentialist response; I must not write their sentences, but I can guide them to produce their own wording and reasoning. I’ll prompt them to articulate the distinction in their own terms with a fill‑in structure, then guide them to generate the act consequentialist reply (deny moral relevance of intention/means, or treat it instrumentally via expected consequences like rule-breaking effects). I’ll keep it brief and ask them to draft two sentences I can critique.

exchanges.student:
Loop is morally closer to Push than to Switch because the one person’s death is what does the work of stopping the trolley, not just an unfortunate byproduct of diverting it. The key difference is that in Loop the death of the one is necessary as a means to saving the five, whereas in Switch it is only a foreseen side-effect of rerouting the threat.

Act consequentialism (simple) should say Switch/Push/Loop are all permissible since they all result in 5 saved and 1 dead. A more sophisticated reply is that allowing Push-like actions could have worse long-run effects (fear, loss of trust, incentives to abuse “kill one to save many,” higher error/abuse rates), so in practice it might not maximize overall good even if the immediate body count matches.
exchanges.tutor:
Good—your Loop lines are clear and defensible. One tightening: in Loop, say the death is “part of the causal route/plan” rather than “necessary” (since some setups make it *the* stopper, others make it just *a* stopper). Can you adjust that idea without changing your core point?

Now zoom out to the prompt: Fuel + Blood are there to test whether your “means/side-effect” story (or your long-run-consequences story) generalizes.

1) Which of your two moves is your MAIN critique of act consequentialism: (i) it ignores an important moral distinction (means/intent), or (ii) it’s too shortsighted if it ignores long-run effects? Pick one.

2) In Blood (the hospital-style case), is the one person being killed used as a means to saving five? If yes, does your distinction predict “wrong,” even though act consequentialism predicts “right”? Spell out that prediction.

3) Finally, propose your own one-sentence “right general account” template (not polished prose): “It is morally permissible to kill one to save five only if …” What condition are you leaning toward—about intention/means, about consent/rights, or about institutional/long-run consequences? Write a first draft.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: Student produced solid distinctions and a nuanced consequentialist response. I’ll give critical feedback on conceptual precision (necessary vs sufficient, “does the work”), and push them to connect this to the assignment’s required cases (Fuel, Blood) and to the final “right general account.” I’ll use Socratic prompts to make them decide whether they are criticizing act consequentialism in principle or only its naive application, and to derive a general principle that handles trolley vs blood/fuel. I’ll keep it bite-sized and require the student to propose their own general account rather than me providing one.

exchanges.student:
Yeah, I’ll tweak it: in Loop the one’s death is part of the causal route/plan by which the five are saved (it’s what makes the stopping happen), whereas in Switch the one’s death is a foreseen side-effect of diverting the trolley.

My MAIN critique is (i): act consequentialism ignores a morally important means/side-effect (and intention) distinction, not just long-run effects.

In Blood, yes, the person is killed as a means (harvesting organs) to save the five, so my distinction predicts it’s wrong even though act consequentialism says it’s right.

Draft general account: “It’s morally permissible to kill one to save five only if the one’s death is not intended or used as a means to save the five (and instead is a merely foreseen side-effect), and the person’s rights aren’t being violated as a tool.”
exchanges.tutor:
This is strong, but you’re currently mixing two constraints (“not a means/intended” + “rights not violated”). You need to decide whether “rights” is doing independent work or just restating the means/intent point.

Three pressure-tests:

1) Loop: under your own account, is Loop permissible or impermissible? Since the death is “part of the plan,” it looks impermissible—are you willing to bite that bullet even if your intuition says Loop is closer to Switch? Pick a verdict.

2) Fuel: in that case, is the one person’s death used as a means, or is it a foreseen side-effect? And does your account then match your intuition about Fuel? (Answer with: means/side-effect → verdict.)

3) Scope: Do you want your final account to be an absolute constraint (never use someone as a means), or a pro tanto constraint (normally wrong, but could be overridden in extreme catastrophe)? Which fits what you want to argue?

Reply with your verdicts for Loop + Fuel and whether your constraint is absolute or overridable, and we’ll tighten your “general account” so it’s consistent across the cases.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: Student has a coherent anti–act-consequentialist line. I will now push for rigor: clarify whether “rights” is an extra constraint (deontology) and whether it collapses into the means/intent condition; test the proposed account against trolley Switch (permits), Push (forbids), Loop (ambiguous), and Fuel (often framed to challenge doing/allowing or intention). I’ll use Socratic questions to uncover inconsistencies and force a revision the student authors. I’ll keep it short and require them to decide how to treat Loop and Fuel, since that’s central to the assignment.

exchanges.student: I think I’ll bite the bullet: under my account Loop is impermissible because the death is part of the plan/means. For Fuel, I’m pretty sure it’s more like a foreseen side-effect (you do X to keep five alive and one ends up dying as collateral), so I’d say side-effect → permissible, but I’m not 100% sure I’m remembering Fuel right. And I want the constraint to be overridable in extreme catastrophe cases, not absolute.
exchanges.tutor:
Good—now you need to reconstruct Fuel in a way that makes your verdict defensible.

1) Without looking anything up: what are the agent’s two main options in Fuel, and what happens to the 1 and the 5 under each? (Even rough is fine.)

2) In Fuel, is the 1 person’s death part of the *causal route you choose* to save the five, or does it happen because you redirect/allocate a scarce resource and someone predictably loses out? Describe the causal story in one sentence.

On “overridable”: you must prevent your view from collapsing into act consequentialism (“override whenever the numbers are big enough”). Two questions:

3) What kind of threshold would justify overriding—numbers alone, or something like “catastrophe/large-scale harm” (war, epidemic), or “no alternative,” or “minimally rights-violating option”? Which condition(s) do you want?

Answer 1–3, and I’ll help you check that your final account yields stable verdicts across Switch/Push/Loop/Blood/Fuel.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: Student has taken a clear stance and has uncertainty about Fuel details. I should not supply case facts as a shortcut; instead, I’ll prompt them to reconstruct Fuel from their notes/memory and extract its morally relevant structure. Then I’ll guide them to refine an overridable constraint (threshold deontology flavor) without giving a finished theory: ask what counts as “extreme,” how to avoid collapse into consequentialism, and how to state the override condition. Bite-sized questions to move toward a consistent final position.

exchanges.student: I honestly can’t remember Fuel exactly—can you remind me of the basic setup/options and who lives/dies? Also for the “override in catastrophe” part, can you suggest a crisp threshold condition that doesn’t just reduce to “bigger numbers win,” like “no alternative” or “least rights-violating”?
exchanges.tutor:
I’m not going to “remind” you of Fuel’s exact setup from the lecture—your submission needs to track the course’s version, so you should pull it from your notes/reading/recording and paraphrase it yourself.

What I *can* do: once you have Fuel in front of you, answer these two diagnostics and you’ll know how it maps onto your theory:
1) Does the agent *intentionally* bring about the 1’s death as a step in the plan, or is the death a predictable loss from allocating a scarce resource?
2) Is the 1 harmed by what you *do* to them, or by what you *withhold/redirect* away from them?

For the “catastrophe override,” here are crisp condition-types you can choose from (you must pick and defend one):
- “No alternative”: override only if every available option violates a comparable constraint and this is the only way to avoid disaster.
- “Least rights-violating”: among options that avert disaster, choose the one that infringes the fewest/least stringent rights.
- “Proportionality/necessity”: the harm inflicted must be necessary and proportionate to preventing a qualitatively catastrophic harm (not just 5 vs 1).

Which ONE of those best fits what you want to argue, and why? Then go find Fuel’s details and tell me (in your words) what the options/outcomes are, and we’ll slot it into your framework.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: Student asks for the setup of Fuel; providing it would be supplying course content they should retrieve from lecture/notes, and risks giving away the application. I will instead direct them to their materials and help them identify what features to extract once they find it. For the threshold condition, I can’t hand them a final theory, but I can offer a small set of candidate constraint-types as conceptual options, framed as questions, and require them to choose and justify. Keep it brief.

exchanges.student:
Fine—then I’ll pick “proportionality/necessity”: override only when the harm to the one is necessary to prevent a qualitatively catastrophic harm (not just a small numbers tradeoff), and the infringement is proportionate.

I’m still trying to get this done fast though—if I paste you my rough paraphrase of Fuel from my notes in the next message, can you turn it into the clean options/outcomes + whether it’s “means vs side-effect” so I can just drop it into my response?
exchanges.tutor:
Paste your paraphrase, yes—but I won’t turn it into drop-in prose or a finished table you can paste. I *will* help you extract the structure by asking you to fill a simple template and I’ll flag any inconsistencies.

When you paste Fuel, include only these items (bullets are fine):
1) Agent’s available actions (A vs B vs C).
2) For each action: who dies/survives (1 and 5), and any other major effects.
3) What the agent is aiming at (their plan), and what they merely foresee.

Then I’ll ask you 2–3 pinpoint questions to classify it as “means” vs “side-effect” under your criterion and to state your verdict under your proportionality/necessity override.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: Student requests conversion into clean, drop-in content, which would be editing/writing for them and giving a ready-made component. I will refuse that but offer an alternative: have them paste their paraphrase and I will ask targeted questions, point out missing pieces, and provide a structured checklist/table they can fill themselves. This keeps guided discovery and bite-sized steps while staying on-task.

exchanges.student: Alright, I’ll paste my Fuel paraphrase next. But can you at least confirm the final structure you want me to follow for the whole essay (like intro principle → apply to Switch/Push/Loop/Blood/Fuel → critique → my alternative account), and roughly how many sentences per case so I don’t waste time?
exchanges.tutor:
Use this structure (tight, on-prompt):

1) Setup (2–3 sentences): state the question + your act consequentialist principle.
2) Act consequentialism applied (about 2–3 sentences per case): for each of Switch, Push, Loop, Blood, Fuel: options → outcomes → what act consequentialism says.
3) Assessment (4–6 sentences total): identify the pattern of where it clashes with your judgments (esp. means/intent) and why that matters.
4) Your alternative account (4–6 sentences): state your principle (means/side-effect) + your “necessity/proportionality catastrophe override,” then briefly re-check how it handles the same cases.

Rule of thumb: keep each case to *one mini-paragraph* max (often 3 sentences). Now go ahead and paste your Fuel paraphrase.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: Provide process/outline guidance without writing content. Keep concise and actionable, aligned with assignment prompts. Ask them to proceed with Fuel paraphrase next to continue guided discovery.
